summary: Dan Shapiro identifies three common mistakes Kickstarter creators make that drive away potential backers: dodging important product details, rushing out stretch goals without proper planning, and downplaying risks. Using a men's clothing project as a case study, he argues that transparency and honesty matter more than polish. He contends that creators should embrace polarizing decisions and be upfront about limitations rather than trying to appeal to everyone. The post ends on a positive note, with the example company overhauling their page in response to feedback.
key_points: Kickstarter creators hurt themselves by avoiding specific product details that might alienate some buyers, when polarizing decisions are actually a startup virtue
Hastily conceived stretch goals signal lack of planning and erode backer confidence in the team's ability to deliver
Most creators treat the 'Risks and Challenges' section as a marketing opportunity rather than an honest assessment, which backfires with savvy backers
Backers don't expect perfection but do expect honesty and transparency about what they're getting
The best response to criticism is listening and adapting, as demonstrated by the featured company's overhaul
money_quotes: Clever, well meaning, adorable, and more clueless than a general with a gmail account.
As a startup, polarizing decisions are a virtue.
If you're selling dryclean-only T-shirts cut for Arnold Schwarzenegger, own it!
When you don't address issues like these proactively, it makes me think you're more concerned with having lots of customers than having happy customers.
It is terrifying to me that you are now accepting real cash money for a product that you conceived of less than 24 hours ago.
Pro tip: if I, who know your business as well as I know the feeding habits of the Springbok Antelope, can come up with more risks than you can, you're not doing it right.
We can forgive a lot, as long as you're being straight with us.
key_insight: Kickstarter backers are taking a bet on your team, and the fastest way to lose that bet is substituting marketing spin for honest transparency about your product, plans, and risks.
